name: Speed up datadog-static-analyzer scan on dd-source

description: Baseline run on main + autoresearch setup files. dd-source via dd-auth, 10/10 fingerprints match.

asi.hypothesis: establish baseline on main with full pipeline (dd-auth + analyzer + 10-repo deep fingerprint check)
asi.notes_for_next_iteration: First optimization to try: prior research's #6 (memchr fast-path in get_lines_to_ignore) — small but trivially safe and fully fingerprint-preserving. Then move to #4 (skip empty-bucket rules early) and #5 (reuse parse tree for is_test_file). Save the structurally-large change (#1 adaptive combined-query) for later when small wins are exhausted.
asi.noise_estimate: back-to-back baselines varied <2s on 250s base; ~1% noise floor

description: memchr fast-path in get_lines_to_ignore: bail when neither no-dd-sa nor datadog-disable appears anywhere in the file. (Prior research item #6.)

asi.hypothesis: vast majority of files have no DD disable marker; bail before the per-line whitespace-stripping + per-pattern .contains() loop
asi.code_change: 5-line fast-path at top of get_lines_to_ignore() using DISABLE_KEYWORDS.iter().any(|kw| code.contains(kw))
asi.why_it_works: 271k files * 2 string scans is much cheaper than 271k files * (line.chars().filter().collect()::<String>() per line + 1-6 .contains() per line). std::str::contains uses SIMD/memchr-optimized two-way search.
asi.next: try #4: skip empty-bucket rules early in the per-rule loop; or #5: reuse parse tree for is_test_file

description: Reuse parsed tree for is_test_file: analyze_with now returns (Vec<RuleResult>, Option<Arc<Tree>>); driver passes the tree to is_test_file instead of None. (Prior research item #5.)

asi.hypothesis: is_test_file re-parses the tree when called with None; reusing the analyze_with-parsed Arc<Tree> avoids 12k re-parses on dd-source
asi.noise_observation: two runs at 249.86 and 237.98 (~12s spread) reveal noise floor is wider than initial ~2s estimate — likely warm/cold page cache or dd-auth jitter. Median of 2 = 243.92.
asi.real_effect_size: negligible-to-tiny-positive on dd-source: most violation-bearing files (12k of 271k) hit has_test_like_path() first (returns early), so the reparse rarely happened anyway
asi.kept_for: API now exposes the parsed tree, enabling future tree-reuse work (e.g. for path_restrictions or downstream classifiers).
asi.next: literal pre-screen #2 — file-level: extract required substrings from #eq?/#any-of? predicates; before parsing/querying, skip language-rules whose literals aren't in the file. Big potential on dd-source where many rules touch zero files.

description: Per-rule + file-level literal pre-screen: extract required #eq?/#any-of? literals from each TS query at startup; skip parse+rule-loop entirely if no rule's literals are present in the file, and skip v8 dispatch per-rule otherwise. (Prior research item #2.)

asi.hypothesis: many of dd-source's ~63 active rules require specific literals (e.g. function names, package names) that are absent in most files. Conservative #eq?/#any-of? extraction lets us skip parse + v8 dispatch on those.
asi.implementation: TSQuery now carries a LiteralPreScreen built from the query source string (own parser — tree-sitter doesn't expose text predicates publicly). Conservative gates: pattern_count==1 only; bail on any '[' outside strings/comments (alternation); skip negative predicates; skip #match? (regex risk); skip capture-vs-capture #eq?.
asi.bug_caught_and_fixed: first version forgot that `[A B]@cap` reports pattern_count==1 even though it's alternation; predicates inside one branch aren't required by the other. Added has_alternation_bracket() bail. Found via dd-git-hooks fingerprint mismatch (go-security/command-injection lost 2 findings).
asi.tests_added: 12 LiteralPreScreen unit tests + 6 alternation-bracket tests; all 338 kernel tests pass.
asi.next: still room — try #1 adaptive combined-query for big-repo files; or extract more from #match? regexes with conservative gates (skip on |, (?i), [, ?, *, +).

description: Extend literal pre-screen to #match? regex predicates: extract longest contiguous literal run, with safety bails on top-level | alternation, (?...) group modifiers, and runs < 3 chars. Unlocks several big-budget rules (dc-url, v1-import-url, fabric.dog) that have only #match? predicates.

asi.hypothesis: many top-time rules (no-hardcoded-dc-url, v1-import-url, fabric.dog) only have #match? predicates with regexes that contain a long literal substring; extracting that substring conservatively unlocks them.
asi.implementation: extract_required_literal_from_regex(): walk regex, treat \X as literal X for X in regex meta-set, treat unescaped meta as run boundaries, pick longest run. Bail on top-level |, on (?...) group modifiers, and on runs <3 chars. Added 6 new tests; 350 kernel tests pass total.
asi.safety_check: fingerprint diff: zero changes vs baseline across all 10 reference repos. Rule output identical.
asi.next_potentially: multi-pattern handling (would unlock dd-trace-go-v2-migration/with-servicename, ~26s CPU, ~3s wall); JS-side const-array mining (would unlock no-hardcoded-cluster Go, ~85s CPU = ~10s wall biggest single rule); adaptive combined query (~2x potential per prior research).

description: Multi-pattern literal pre-screen: extract per-pattern (using start_byte_for_pattern / end_byte_for_pattern) and OR across patterns. Unlocks rules with multiple top-level patterns where every pattern is constrained (e.g. dd-trace-go-v2-migration/with-servicename: P1=#match? gopkg.in/...DataDog/dd-trace-go.v1/.* + P2=#eq? WithServiceName). Bails if any pattern is unconstrained.

asi.hypothesis: many top-time rules have 2+ top-level patterns, each requiring a literal. Single-pattern bail was leaving these on the table. Per-pattern extraction with cross-pattern OR captures them.
asi.implementation: LiteralPreScreen now stores Vec<Vec<Vec<String>>> = OR-over-patterns of AND-of-OR. extract_from_query() iterates query.pattern_count() and slices source via start/end_byte_for_pattern; each pattern's predicates extracted independently. Bail conditions: zero patterns; any pattern with [] alternation; any pattern with no extractable AND-groups (unconstrained → OR of patterns is unconstrained).
asi.tests_added: 3 multi-pattern tests using real Go grammar (both-constrained, one-unconstrained-bails, alternation-bracket-bails); 353 kernel tests pass total.
asi.rules_unlocked_examples: dd-trace-go-v2-migration/with-servicename (was 27s CPU on 100k Go files), with-withdogstatsaddress (26s)
asi.next: remaining big consumers: no-hardcoded-cluster (Go) at 87s CPU — needs JS-side mining but the rule does prefix matching (cluster.match(/^[a-z0-9]+/i)[0]), so naive array mining would miss findings. cluster-yml/datacenter-yml have [] alternation, also need JS mining. Combined query (#1) is the next big lever — estimated 2x potential for big workloads per prior research.
asi.noise: build_seconds 20.81 = full kernel rebuild after adding many lines

description: Smarter literal pre-screen: track [-depth and ?/*/+ quantifier suffixes precisely instead of bailing on any [-bracket. Predicates inside alternation or quantified groups are skipped; predicates at depth 0 in unquantified groups still extracted. Unlocks rules with localized [...] alternation but top-level required literals (e.g. python-flask/cookie-injection's #eq? set_cookie outside its inner alternation).

asi.hypothesis: many rules have a localized [...] alternation (e.g. for accepting either node form A or B) but a separate, unconditional predicate at depth 0 elsewhere in the pattern. The previous coarse 'any [' bail missed these. Tracking [-depth and quantifier suffixes lets us extract the always-required predicates while still skipping the conditional ones.
asi.implementation: two-pass walk of pattern source: pass 1 collects all (#xxx?) predicates with their open-paren stack; tracks open-paren positions whose closing ')' is followed by ?/*/+; pass 2 keeps only predicates at bracket-depth 0 whose enclosing opens are not in the conditional set. Removed coarse has_alternation_bracket bail.
asi.remaining_top_consumers: no-hardcoded-cluster (Go) 88s CPU — needs JS-side mining (risky due to JS prefix matching). Combined query (#1) is the next big lever.

description: Verification rerun (no code changes) — confirms 155-157s range stably reproduces. Current best holds.

asi.hypothesis: session noise floor confirmation: same binary, fresh run
asi.remarks: back-to-back wall delta 155.27 vs 156.65 = ~1s noise floor on 156s metric. confidence in 33-38% range improvements. Two runs at the same code: 155.27 / 156.65 → spread 1.4s.
asi.next_strategic_thinking: remaining big consumers are JS-heavy (no-hardcoded-cluster, datacenter) where TS-query-based screening can't help. Combined query (#1) would save ~3-5s wall for the shared interpreted_string_literal traversal but needs multi-hour surgery. JS-side const-array mining is unsafe for cluster rules due to JS prefix-extraction transforms (cluster.match(/^[a-z0-9]+/i)) — conservative gates per prior research would block this rule. Plateauing at -38%.

description: Pre-compute per-rule applicability mask once and reuse in the per-rule loop instead of re-evaluating each screen. Discarded: regressed wall by ~10s (164.93 vs 155.27).

asi.hypothesis: deduping redundant pre_screen.matches() between file-level any() and per-rule loop would save CPU; in practice it cost more.
asi.rollback_reason: +10s wall vs prev best. The previous .any() short-circuited on the FIRST passing rule — typical, since most files contain at least one common literal. The mask version eagerly evaluates ALL N rules' screens for EVERY file (no short-circuit), then allocates a Vec<bool>. Upfront work + alloc > saved redundant calls.
asi.next_action_hint: Don't replace short-circuit-friendly any() patterns with mask precomputation when the per-element cost is high. Keep the redundant per-rule check.
asi.fingerprints_unchanged: 10/10 match; correctness preserved — regression is purely perf.
asi.lesson: fast-path heuristics (any() short-circuiting, lazy evaluation) often beat eager precomputation when the early-exit case is common. Profile before refactoring the screen evaluation path.

description: JS-side const array literal mining (#3): for top-level `const NAME = [...]` arrays in rule JS code, mine each element as required (full + leading [a-zA-Z0-9_]+ prefix) and inject into the rule's pre-screen as a global OR-group. Conservative safety gates per prior research + extras. Unlocks no-hardcoded-cluster (Go) which was 89s CPU per scan.

asi.hypothesis: biggest single rule (no-hardcoded-cluster Go @ 89s CPU) has a top-level `const clusters = ['name.suffix.suffix.suffix', ...]` array. JS does both `match.includes(cluster)` (full) and `match.includes(cluster.match(/^[a-z0-9]+/i)[0])` (prefix). Mining BOTH the full element AND its leading-alphanumeric run safely covers both branches.
asi.implementation: new analysis/js_literal_miner.rs (~330 lines + 11 tests). Walks JS code tracking brace/paren depth, finds top-level const/let/var = [strings...] declarations. Safety gates: name no TYPE/KIND/NODE; at least one element non-alphanumeric (rules out AST type lists); name referenced elsewhere in JS (>1 occurrence); TS query has no [ outside strings/comments. For each element: emit both the full string and its leading [a-zA-Z0-9_]+ run when each is >=3 chars. LiteralPreScreen.add_global_required attaches them as a global AND-group on top of any TS-derived constraints.
asi.first_attempt_bug: v1 of the reference check (`code[after_decl..].contains(name)`) failed for arrays declared AFTER the visit() function (cluster rule's case). Switched to occurrence count > 1 across the whole file.
asi.what_else_was_unlocked: this also unlocks any other rule following the same pattern — cluster rules in other languages (-py, -starlark, -rust, -java) and various python-pandas/django rules with broad-capture queries + JS arrays.

description: Relax JS-mining's alternation gate: when TS query has [...] alternation BUT the rule's JS body references exactly one capture name (uniform handling across branches), allow JS array mining to proceed. Unlocks no-hardcoded-datacenter (Go) at 84s CPU and the YML cluster/datacenter rules. Verified again at 71.93s.

asi.hypothesis: the original `TS query has [` bail blocked too many rules from JS mining. Cluster/datacenter rules use TS alternation but ALL branches capture as a single `@literal` and the JS uniformly checks `query.captures.literal`. Counting distinct query.captures.<NAME> references in JS lets us prove the JS treats all branches the same way, which is sufficient for safety.
asi.implementation: added distinct_js_capture_count() that scans for query.captures.X / query.captures["X"] / capturesList variants and counts distinct names. mine_required_literals proceeds despite TS alternation iff exactly 1 distinct capture is referenced.
asi.verification_run: 73.17s, then 71.93s back-to-back — stable in 72-73s range, ~80x noise floor.
asi.tests_added: skips_when_ts_query_has_alternation_and_multiple_js_captures, allows_alternation_when_js_uses_single_capture; 12 miner tests + 350 base = 362 total kernel tests pass.
asi.rules_unlocked_estimate: no-hardcoded-datacenter (Go, 84s CPU saved), no-hardcoded-cluster-yml, no-hardcoded-datacenter-yml — all use single-capture JS uniformly across alternation branches.

description: Detect patterns whose @captures aren't referenced in JS body and exclude them from pre-screen (so an unused unconstrained pattern doesn't force the screen to bail). Discarded: neutral on dd-source (74.26 / 72.68 vs prev best 71.93, within noise) — added code complexity without measurable gain.

asi.hypothesis: rules like python-flask/command-injection have an unused auxiliary pattern that prevents pre-screen extraction. Detecting JS-unused patterns and dropping them from screen-building should let pattern 1's `#eq? "subprocess"` constrain the screen.
asi.rollback_reason: neutral on dd-source. Two runs at 74.26/72.68s vs prev best 71.93s — within ~3s noise floor. Likely "subprocess" appears in most Python files in dd-source, so the screen barely filters anything for python-flask/command-injection.
asi.next_action_hint: diminishing returns: top-time consumers now have screens that don't filter much because the required literals are common. Further gains likely need either combined-query (shared traversal across rules) or per-file overhead reductions (file walking, parse cost), not more screening cleverness.
asi.code_lessons: the architecture (rebuild_pre_screen_filtered + js_capture_references + pattern_capture_names) is clean and could be reused by future ideas; just doesn't help THIS workload.

description: Verification rerun (no code changes). Range 71.93–74.26 confirms stable plateau at ~72s. Logging as discard since no improvement vs prev best.

asi.hypothesis: session noise floor at the new plateau
asi.rollback_reason: no code change; primary metric did not improve. Logging as observation only.
asi.next_action_hint: remaining wall is dominated by per-file overhead (parsing trees, file walking, classification) and the small long tail of remaining rules. Real further gains likely need either: (a) parallelism scaling (current default caps at min(num_cpus, 8), using ~6 worker threads of 16 available; lifting that cap could nearly halve analyzer Duration); (b) combined Tree-sitter query for shared traversal (~5s wall), (c) language-level parallelism (current code serializes across languages — Go finishes before Python starts). All of these are structural changes outside the conservative spirit of this session.

description: Language-level parallelism: replace the sequential `for language in languages` loop in `static_analysis()` with a rayon `par_iter()`. Each language's per-file fold/reduce still runs file-parallel; nested rayon shares the global thread pool via work-stealing. Setup (filter_files, convert_rules) stays serial to surface errors early. Progress bars disabled in parallel mode (would interleave). 2 runs at 70.94/70.43 vs prev plateau 71.9-74.3.

asi.hypothesis: the for-language loop serializes wall: Go finishes → Python starts → YAML starts. Smaller languages don't fully saturate the rayon pool. Parallelizing across languages lets the small ones run during Go's tail, lowering wall to ~max(per-language) instead of sum.
asi.implementation: collect (language, files, rules) triples sequentially; then `.into_par_iter().map(|(lang, files, rules)| inner-fold-reduce).collect()`; finally merge results sequentially (no Mutex needed). Progress bar removed in parallel mode (would interleave).
asi.real_effect_size: ~1.5s wall on top of prev best. Smaller than my a-priori estimate (~6s) because dd-source's Go pass dominates so heavily that the other languages mostly fit into Go's tail without affecting wall.
asi.next: diminishing returns territory. Other ideas in autoresearch.ideas.md.

description: Parallelize setup phase (filter_files + convert_rules) across languages with rayon par_iter. Discarded: regressed wall by 5-10s (75.39, 80.86 vs prev best 70.43).

asi.hypothesis: setup phase (filter 271k file paths + compile per-language rule queries) is currently serial, ~1-3s. Parallelizing should overlap with the start of analysis.
asi.rollback_reason: regressed wall by 5-10s consistently across 2 runs. Possible cause: nested rayon par_iter (setup-parallel + per-language-parallel + per-file-parallel) over-subscribes the thread pool and creates scheduling contention. Or: the printlns interleave and add stdout-lock contention. Or: setup work moves into the worker pool, delaying file analysis.
asi.next_action_hint: keep setup serial — it's small and not on the critical path. Don't nest rayon more than 2 deep.

description: Lift DEFAULT_MAX_CPUS from 8 to 16. The conservative cap was leaving 50% of cores idle on multi-core dev/CI hardware (16-core box used 6 worker threads). On 4/8-core machines, choose_cpu_count's `min(logical_cores, cap)` clamps back to logical_cores, so smaller machines are unaffected.

asi.hypothesis: the analyzer is CPU-bound by the rayon worker count. Doubling the cap from 8 to 16 unblocks ~13 effective worker threads (per get_num_threads_to_use) on 16-core hardware.
asi.memory_concern: peak_rss_mb 1336 vs baseline 909 = +47% (above the 25% red-flag threshold). Well-understood cause: each rayon worker holds its own v8 isolate + per-rule script cache; memory roughly scales linearly with worker count. 1.3 GB is acceptable for dev/CI hardware that has 16+ logical cores (≥ 8GB RAM is reasonable to expect there). Keeping per the resource-gate judgment: 'spike is well-understood, wall-time win is large.' Smaller machines unaffected because choose_cpu_count clamps to min(logical_cores, cap).
asi.behavioral_change_warning: this is a default-value change that affects ALL users invoking the analyzer without `--cpus`. Memory-constrained CI environments may want to set `--cpus 8` explicitly to preserve the prior behavior.
asi.next_action_hint: with ~13 worker threads now in play, the per-worker cost (v8 isolate setup, JS script compilation per rule) becomes more visible. Lazy v8 isolate creation per thread is already in place via thread_local.

description: Remove the `* 0.9` headroom factor in get_num_threads_to_use, lifting worker count from 13 to 15 on 16-core host. Discarded: only ~1s improvement (54.72/54.98 vs prev best 55.87), within noise. The original comment specifically mentioned reserving headroom for management threads — preserving original design intent.

asi.hypothesis: removing the 0.9 conservative factor would unlock more parallelism
asi.rollback_reason: wall improvement ~1s on 56s base = 1.7%; noise floor is ~1-2s in this regime so improvement is borderline. Plus the original comment explicitly called out reserving headroom for management threads — changing the design intent for marginal gain isn't worth it.
asi.next_action_hint: we're hitting hardware limits (96% CPU utilization at 13 workers). Further gains need either combined query (shared traversal across rules), per-file overhead reductions, or rule-side changes.

description: Adaptive combined Tree-sitter query (per language). When file_count*rule_count > 10000, build ONE combined `tree_sitter::Query` covering all rules' patterns and walk each file's tree once, dispatching matches to per-rule v8 via new `JsRuntime::execute_rule_with_matches`. Below the threshold, falls back to per-rule path so small repos avoid the upfront combined-query build cost. (Prior research item #1, made adaptive.)

asi.hypothesis: many rules' Tree-sitter queries traverse overlapping parts of the parse tree (e.g. python-flask rules all hit `function_definition` ancestors). Combining them into a single multi-pattern query lets tree-sitter walk the tree once and emit matches for whichever pattern fires at each node — amortizing traversal across rules.
asi.implementation: new analysis/combined_query.rs (~250 LOC + 2 tests). Stores tree_sitter::Query + capture_names + pattern_to_rule_idx mapping. matches_per_rule() walks the tree, buckets matches by pattern_index → rule_idx. New JsRuntime::execute_rule_with_matches public wrapper accepts pre-computed matches and skips internal TS query phase. New analyze_with_combined() in analyze.rs uses both. Driver in bins/lib.rs picks combined vs per-rule path based on file_count*rule_count > 10_000.
asi.adaptiveness: tested with various sizes; below threshold the upfront Query::new cost dominates (1150-rule combined query takes ~hundreds of ms). dd-source's Go (100k files * 108 Go rules = 10.8M, way above threshold) is exactly the case combined query was designed for.
asi.tests_added: builds_combined_query, dispatches_matches_to_correct_rules; full kernel tests (362) still pass.
asi.complete_session_arc: this is the prior research's #1 (largest single win). Made adaptive per the original recommendation. Stack on top of: literal pre-screen (#2), JS const-array mining (#3), language-level rayon parallelism, CPU-cap raise.

description: Final-state verification rerun (no code changes). 5-run mean was 48.25s; this run at 50.39s is at the upper edge of the 47-50s warm-cache range. Confirms steady-state.

asi.hypothesis: verify steady state after combined query is in
asi.rollback_reason: no code change; metric at top of warm range (50.4 vs 5-run mean 48.25). Logging as observation only.
asi.session_complete: 12 keeps including this verification, 6 discards (5 perf, 1 verification of existing state). Total improvement −80.8 % on dd-source. All ideas with reasonable ROI explored — see autoresearch.ideas.md for the deferred ones (parallel directory walking with jwalk, threshold tuning).
asi.remaining_potential: marginal: parallel walking ~1-3s; threshold tuning ~0-2s; combined query timing accounting (cosmetic). All require either new deps or borderline judgment calls.

description: Lower combined-query threshold from 10000 to 1000. Discarded: dd-source (271k × 63 = 17M) is far above either threshold so behavior is identical; the change matters only for smaller repos which aren't part of the primary benchmark.

asi.hypothesis: lowering the threshold would extend combined-query benefits to medium-sized workloads
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable effect on dd-source (47.49 / 48.62 vs prev best 47.38 — within noise). The change is potentially better for medium repos but can't be measured against this benchmark; per protocol, no metric improvement → discard.
asi.next_action_hint: if a future benchmark targeting medium repos is added, revisit threshold tuning. Current 10000 is a reasonable conservative default.

description: Thread-local QueryCursor reuse in analyze_with_combined to avoid 271k cursor allocations. Discarded: 48.33s vs prev best 47.38s — within noise (5-run mean 48.25, spread 0.6). Confirms prior research's "thread-local Parser cache had no measurable win" — cursor allocation is similarly cheap relative to the actual tree walk.

asi.hypothesis: 271k QueryCursor::new() calls per scan have measurable allocator overhead. Reusing via thread_local should save the allocation cost.
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable wall improvement on dd-source. Cursor allocation is dominated by the larger tree-walk traversal cost, just like Parser allocation per the prior research. tree-sitter's internal cursor state is small enough that the per-call setup is essentially free relative to the walk itself.
asi.next_action_hint: skip allocator-cache micro-optimizations; remaining wins are likely structural (parallel directory walking with jwalk, rule-side changes, language-level batching).

description: Skip empty-bucket and screen-failed `RuleResult` emission in `analyze_with_combined` (prior research item #4). Discarded: 49.02 / 49.23 / 49.96 s vs prev best 47.38 — consistent ~2 s slower. user_cpu_seconds rose 343 → 365 (+22 s) which is the opposite of expected (skipping allocations should reduce CPU). Possibly a memory-layout / cache-locality regression: smaller per-file `Vec<RuleResult>` may interact worse with rayon's reduce step's `.extend()` reallocation pattern.

asi.hypothesis: skipping ~13 M empty-RuleResult allocations across the scan should reduce CPU and wall.
asi.rollback_reason: consistent ~2s regression across 3 runs (49.02, 49.23, 49.96) and +22 s user_cpu. Counterintuitive direction — needs investigation before retrying. Plausible explanations: (a) rayon's fold/reduce extend() Vec reallocation hits a bad capacity-doubling step at the smaller results sizes; (b) the previous larger-and-then-shrunk Vec<RuleResult> was actually pre-warming the heap allocator's free-list in a way that made subsequent allocs cheaper.
asi.next_action_hint: the win was 'easy' according to prior research — maybe their measurement was on a different rule mix or older codebase. Don't reattempt without first profiling allocator behavior. Move on.

description: Drop the per-rule pre_screen check in `analyze_with_combined`, relying on bucket-empty alone. Discarded: major regression 59.54 s vs prev best 47.38 s (+12 s wall, +160 s user_cpu). The pre-screen check is NOT redundant — even though combined-query produces no matches when pre_screen rejects, the screen also prevents v8 dispatches in cases where pre_screen rejects but combined-query DOES produce matches (rules with broader patterns where the pre_screen literals aren't strictly required for tree-sitter match but are required for the rule to actually fire in JS). Removing the check forces v8 to do extra work on those files, which then produce no findings.

asi.hypothesis: since pre_screen-fail implies the combined-query bucket is empty (by construction of literal screening), the per-rule pre-screen check after combined-query dispatch is redundant.
asi.rollback_reason: +12 s wall, +160 s user_cpu. The premise was wrong: pre_screen and the combined-query pattern are NOT in 1:1 correspondence in all cases. A file might have non-empty matches (TS query fires) but the rule's pre-screen fails because the JS-side mining adds literals beyond what the TS query strictly requires. Removing the pre-screen check sends those files through v8 even though the rule semantically can't fire — v8 churns matches and produces no findings. Fingerprints stay correct (10/10) but CPU explodes.
asi.lesson_learned: the pre_screen check serves TWO purposes in combined-query path: (a) avoid the empty-RuleResult allocation, (b) avoid redundant v8 dispatch when pre_screen rejects but TS pattern still matches (e.g. JS-mined literals tighten the pre-screen beyond the TS query's strict requirements). DON'T remove it.
asi.next_action_hint: the pre_screen check is doing more than I thought. Move on to non-pre-screen-related optimizations.

description: Pre-filter combined-query matches by hoisting the per-rule pre-screen check upfront via `accept_rule: Vec<bool>`, then passing it as a mask to `matches_per_rule_filtered`. Discarded: 49.03 / 49.15 vs best 47.38 (+1.7s wall, +21s user_cpu). Same regression pattern as run #9 — eager precomputation of all rules' pre-screens loses the lazy `.any()` short-circuit at the file-level filter (in the same `analyze_with_combined`, the `any_rule_could_match` check below evaluates ALL rules sequentially due to the eager mask).

asi.hypothesis: skipping match-tuple construction for screen-rejected rules in the combined-query path saves per-match capture-Vec allocations.
asi.rollback_reason: eager pre-screen evaluation of all rules per file (Vec<bool> map.collect()) costs ~21 s CPU more than the existing lazy-but-short-circuiting evaluation pattern. The savings from skipping screen-rejected match construction don't recover that.
asi.matches_pattern_run_9: this is structurally the same dead end as run #9: lazy any() short-circuit > eager precomputation when most files exit the filter quickly. The combined-query path already pays the per-rule check inside the filter_map loop, where it short-circuits on rule-disabled / pre_screen-fail without doing the full mask up front.
asi.next_action_hint: lazy beats eager. Don't replace any() with map().collect() patterns. Try fundamentally different angles (rule-level batching, language-batching) or accept the plateau.

description: Reorder per-rule loop in `analyze_with_combined`: check bucket-empty BEFORE pre_screen (cheaper check first; defer pre_screen for files where TS pattern fired). Discarded: 48.91 / 47.86 vs 5-run-best mean 48.25 — within noise, no measurable win. Adds explanatory comment about the unusual ordering without payoff.

asi.hypothesis: bucket.is_empty() is cheaper than pre_screen.matches(code); reordering should save pre_screen calls for the common case (most rules empty bucket per file).
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable improvement. Two runs at 48.91/47.86, mean 48.39 vs prev 48.25. The savings are real (we skip pre_screen calls when bucket is empty) but small enough to be lost in noise on a 48s base. The added complication (pre_screen check AFTER mem::take is non-obvious; needs a comment explaining JS-mined-literals-tighten-screen) isn't worth it for no measurable gain.
asi.next_action_hint: we're firmly plateaued. Recent runs show mean wall ~48s with ~1s noise. Real wins require structural changes outside scope (new deps, default-value tweaks).

description: Baseline verification (no code changes after run #25 revert). 47.86s wall + 342.6s user_cpu — matches run #18 (47.38 / 343) within noise. Confirms the +20s CPU regressions seen in runs #22, #24, #25 were real (not system drift), and that the current commit is at the established plateau.

asi.hypothesis: verify session plateau; check whether system load has shifted to invalidate prior measurements
asi.rollback_reason: no code change; logged for measurement only
asi.plateau_reaffirmed: 47.86s with user_cpu 342.59 — essentially identical to run #18 (47.38, 343). The plateau is genuine. Recent eager-mask and empty-RuleResult-skip experiments WERE real ~+20s CPU regressions, not system noise.
asi.session_state: 26 experiments, 13 keep / 12 discard / 1 measurement-only. Best 47.38s (run #18). Mean ~48.25s. Plateau confirmed across multiple sessions.
asi.next_action_hint: honest stop signal. Further wins require relaxing constraints (new deps, behavior changes) or hardware changes. The autoresearch.ideas.md backlog captures the deferred high-potential items if those constraints relax.

description: Parallelize file walking with depth-2 fan-out + parallelize filter_files_by_size. Both use a *local* rayon ThreadPool (since the global one isn't initialized until later in main, after initialize_v8). Two-level fan-out is critical for monorepos like dd-source where one mid-tree directory (`domains/`) holds ~95% of files: without depth-2, only the top 23 dirs run in parallel and `domains/` becomes a sequential bottleneck. With depth-2, we surface 200+ leaf subtrees that map cleanly onto rayon workers.

asi.hypothesis: the 18s of post-analyzer overhead is dominated by sequential file walking via WalkDir on 272k files. Adding instrumentation revealed get_files takes 13s and filter_files_by_size takes 2.5s.
asi.implementation: (a) get_files: read top-level entries, then for each subdirectory ALSO read its immediate entries, fan out depth-2 subdirs as parallel work-list. Local ThreadPool (sized to num_cpus) sidesteps the global pool which is initialized later in main. (b) filter_files_by_size: par_iter over files for the metadata + is_file syscalls, also via a local pool.
asi.depth_iteration_story: first try (single-level fan-out) only saved ~2s because dd-source/domains/ is one giant subtree that became a bottleneck. depth-2 fan-out saves ~10s by spreading domains/'s ~255 sub-subdirs across all 16 cores.
asi.memory_note: peak_rss DROPPED from 1348 → 1198 MB. The parallel walk seems to allocate slightly less than the single-threaded walk (probably due to faster Vec growth pattern with smaller per-thread Vec accumulators).
asi.scope_check: this is a generally-useful optimization, not a benchmark-specific tweak. ANY large-monorepo scan benefits.

description: Drop the conservative `* 0.9` factor in get_num_threads_to_use, lifting analyzer's rayon worker count from 13 to 15 on 16-core hosts. Previously discarded in run #18 as within noise; retried after run #27 (parallel file walking) shifted where the bottleneck sits. Now gives a real 5% wall improvement (35.48 → 33.68) because rayon's analyzer pool is the dominant CPU consumer once file walking is no longer the long pole.

asi.hypothesis: the * 0.9 conservative factor is leaving headroom that work-stealing can fill. Run #18 saw ~1s improvement (within noise then) but the file-walking bottleneck was masking the impact. Now that walking is parallelized, more analyzer threads = more analyzer parallelism.
asi.verification: two runs at 33.70 / 33.68, mean 33.69, spread 0.02s. Highly stable.
asi.memory_note: peak_rss rose 1198 → 1417 MB (+18%). 2 more workers × each holding its own v8 isolate + script cache. Still well under the resource-gate threshold.
asi.lesson_learned: discards aren't always permanent — the same change can succeed after a different bottleneck is removed. Re-trying previously-noise-floor changes after a major shift in the workload structure is worthwhile.

description: Pre-index rules by id in a HashMap for SARIF generate_results lookups instead of linear scan. Discarded: 34.20 / 35.05 vs prev best 33.68 — within noise (~1s). The expected savings (~280ms estimated) were lost in benchmark variance.

asi.hypothesis: linear scan rules.iter().position() per RuleResult is O(N×M); HashMap should make it O(1).
asi.rollback_reason: no measurable improvement on dd-source; two runs at 34.20/35.05 vs prev 33.68. Estimated savings (~280ms) were below the ~1s noise floor at this metric level. The optimization is *correct* and theoretically faster; just not visible above noise.
asi.next_action_hint: for sub-second wins on a 33s metric, need a tighter benchmark (multiple runs, lower variance) to see effects. Sticking with current best.

description: Stability verification (no code changes after run #29 revert). 33.60 s vs prev best 33.68 s — within ±0.1 s of three measurements (33.60/33.68/33.70). Plateau is genuine and tight; further wins below the ~1 s noise floor are essentially unmeasurable on this benchmark setup.

asi.hypothesis: verify the 33.68s plateau holds with the current code
asi.rollback_reason: no code change; logged for measurement only
asi.plateau_stability: 33.60 / 33.68 / 33.70 across 3 separate runs (after run #28 keep). Spread <0.1s. Noise is much tighter than the earlier 1-2s spread — perhaps because warmer caches and stable CPU thermals at ~33s wall. Mean = 33.66s.
asi.session_state: 30 experiments, 14 keeps / 15 discards / 1 measurement-only. Best 33.68 -> ~33.6 plateau. -86.6% wall vs 251s baseline.
asi.last_explorations: tried HashMap optimization for SARIF (run #29: lost in noise); SARIF parallelization blocked by Rc<Repository> non-Send; GlobSet conversion would be marginal (~0.3s wall) but requires non-trivial refactor.
asi.next_action_hint: for the user to decide. Below the noise floor here. Promising but unimplemented: GlobSet for path config (small win, refactor cost), SARIF generate_results parallelization (blocked by current type system, would need Repository -> Arc<Repository>), parallel rule conversion at startup (tiny, ~50ms).

description: Fold size filter into get_files (use WalkDir's cached metadata) to skip the second pass through 272k files in filter_files_by_size. Discarded: 3 runs at 33.59/34.54/34.13 (mean 34.09) vs prev plateau 33.66 — slight regression. Sys_cpu DID drop by ~4s (real syscall savings) but wall didn't improve because the previous filter_files_by_size used `par_iter` over the flat 271k-entry Vec, giving better load balancing across rayon workers than the inline size check during the unbalanced subtree walk (where dd-source's `domains/` subtree dominates).

asi.hypothesis: second pass through 271k files in filter_files_by_size is redundant work; folding into the walk should save the pass.
asi.rollback_reason: syscall savings real (~4s sys_cpu drop, consistent across 3 runs) but wall is +0.4s slower on average. The filter_files_by_size par_iter over the flat-Vec evenly distributes work across all 16 threads. The inline size check in get_files_with_size_limit happens during the depth-2 subtree walk where load is unbalanced (`domains/` is 95% of dd-source). Net wash on wall, slight regression on noise.
asi.lesson_learned: second-pass filter pattern can be more wall-efficient than inline filter when the second pass has better load balancing. Keep filter_files_by_size as a separate par_iter step.
asi.next_action_hint: the file walk + size filter is fine as-is. Look elsewhere for wins.

description: Move v8 init + rayon global pool init BEFORE get_files/filter_files_by_size, so they can use the global pool instead of building short-lived local pools. Discarded: 33.89 / 34.20 (mean 34.05) vs prev 33.66 — slight regression. Likely cause: global pool has 15 threads (get_num_threads_to_use(16) = 15) while the prior local pools used num_cpus::get() = 16 — one less worker for walking offsets the saved pool-spawn overhead.

asi.hypothesis: sharing the rayon global pool across all phases (file walk + filter + analysis) eliminates 2 short-lived local pool spawns (~30 ms saved).
asi.rollback_reason: slight regression. Global pool sizing (15) is smaller than the local pools' size (16 = num_cpus::get()), so file walking lost one worker thread — net wash or slightly negative on wall, even though we saved local-pool setup overhead.
asi.lesson_learned: the file-walk and analyzer phases benefit from DIFFERENT thread counts. Walking is syscall-bound and benefits from saturating num_cpus. Analyzer is CPU-bound and benefits from leaving 1 core for v8 watchdog. Sharing one pool sized for analyzer hurts walking slightly. Worth keeping separate pools (or using num_cpus for walking).
asi.next_action_hint: if revisited: use a separate larger local pool just for walking, keep global pool sized for analysis. But that's basically the current architecture, so accept the plateau.

description: Try `num_cpus` (16 workers) instead of `num_cpus - 1` (15 workers) on 16-core host. Discarded: 3 runs at 34.67/33.61/34.88 (mean 34.39) vs plateau 33.66 — consistent ~0.7s regression. The v8 watchdog thread competes with rayon worker threads for the 16th physical core when all are saturated, causing scheduling contention. The original `num_cpus - 1` correctly leaves headroom.

asi.hypothesis: use all 16 cores for analyzer workers since v8 watchdog runs on a separate thread that wouldn't compete heavily.
asi.rollback_reason: regression of ~0.7s wall across 3 verification runs. The v8 watchdog thread DOES contend for cycles with the 16th rayon worker on a 16-core host once analyzer threads saturate. The previous 15-worker setting was correctly accounting for this; reverting to `num_cpus - 1`.
asi.session_state: 33 experiments, 14 keeps, 18 discards. Best 33.68s. Plateau remains at 33.6-33.7s.
asi.lesson_learned: the v8 watchdog interaction was REAL despite my earlier hypothesis. Always leave one core free for management threads on multi-threaded analyzers.

description: Replace per-pattern glob iteration in get_files with a single GlobSet for ignore + only patterns. Discovered via instrumentation: path filtering was ~17s CPU during file walk (~1s wall) because each entry iterated ~135 patterns sequentially. GlobSet bulk matches all patterns in one SIMD-optimized pass.

asi.hypothesis: instrumentation showed path_filter takes 17s CPU summed across walk_subtree calls (single subtree at 0.94s for cloud_platform). PathPattern.matches iterated ~135 ignore patterns per dirEntry; ~272k entries × 135 patterns × ~500ns/pattern ≈ 17s CPU. A single GlobSet match is ~300ns regardless of pattern count.
asi.verification_runs: 3 back-to-back: 33.41 / 33.10 / 33.16, mean 33.22 (vs prev plateau mean 33.66, spread 0.31).
asi.implementation: added build_path_glob_set() helper in cli/src/file_utils.rs that compiles a list of PathPatterns into a GlobSet. In get_files, build the ignore + only GlobSets once at function entry, then use them inside walk_subtree. Prefix-based matching (PathPattern.prefix) is preserved as a separate fallback. Required adding `globset = "0.4.16"` as a direct dep on the cli crate (already a transitive dep via kernel, so binary size unchanged).
asi.scope_check: this is a generally-useful optimization: any analyzer scan with many ignore patterns (gitignore + DEFAULT_IGNORED_GLOBS + custom ignore) benefits, not just dd-source. Larger repos with longer pattern lists would benefit even more.
asi.lesson_repeated: instrumentation reveals hotspots that micro-optimization speculation misses. Spent 7+ iterations trying to push past the 33.6s plateau before adding fine-grained timing inside walk_subtree.

description: Reorder file-level pre-screen check: evaluate `pre_screen.matches(code)` BEFORE `rule_config.rule_is_enabled(name)` in the per-rule any() filter. The pre-screen is a cheap memchr-style content check; rule_is_enabled walks path_restrictions (HashMap + glob iteration). On dd-source most rules are config-enabled but many files trip pre_screen for SOME rules but not all — putting pre_screen first lets .any() short-circuit on the cheaper check, saving rule_is_enabled work for screen-rejected rules.

asi.hypothesis: instrumentation showed the file-level any() screen takes ~7s wall (~106s CPU summed across 15 threads). Per (file, rule): pre_screen first if cheaper, then rule_is_enabled.
asi.verification: two runs at 32.63 / 32.50, mean 32.57. Highly stable.
asi.implementation: swap the AND order from `rule_is_enabled && pre_screen` to `pre_screen && rule_is_enabled`. .any() short-circuits at the first passing rule on the cheaper check.
asi.instrumentation_methodology_paid_off_aga: third major win this session from instrumenting first then optimizing. Run #27 (file walking 13s→20s wall via instrumentation), run #34 (path filtering 17s→1s wall via GlobSet), run #35 (screen reorder 7s−6s wall via cheap-check-first). All discovered by adding fine-grained timers, not by speculating.
asi.lesson: after each major optimization, re-profile. Bottlenecks shift.
